channel.title: Longarm Quilting in Colorado Springs, CO | Stitch Spot
channel.description: Longarm Quilting in Colorado Springs, CO

image.title: Longarm Quilting in Colorado Springs, CO

item.description:
Quilt Type Pages — SEO Titles & Descriptions

What this folder does

The portfolio has filter categories — Custom, Edge-to-Edge, and Made-to-Order. Each category gets its own page on the site (for example, stitch.spot/portfolio/custom/). These pages are created automatically by Hugo, the tool that builds the website.

The problem: Hugo names those pages using the short category label alone, so Google would see a title like “Custom | Stitch Spot” — too brief to rank well in search results.

The small files in this folder give each category page a proper, descriptive title and a short summary that search engines display beneath the link. Nothing else on the site changes — the URLs stay the same, the portfolio grid stays the same.

What each file does

File What it controls
_index.md Prevents Hugo from publishing a page at /quilt_type/ (visitors never need to see that path)
custom/_index.md Title & summary for the Custom portfolio page
edge-to-edge/_index.md Title & summary for the Edge-to-Edge portfolio page
made-to-order/_index.md Title & summary for the Made-to-Order portfolio page

How to edit a title or description

Open the file for the category you want to change (e.g. custom/_index.md). Inside you’ll see three fields between the --- lines:

title: "Custom Longarm Quilting — Colorado Springs, CO"
slug: custom
description: "Custom longarm quilting tailored to your quilt's design. …"
  • title — what appears in the browser tab and in Google search results.
  • slug — the URL segment. Keep this identical to the folder name so the address doesn’t change.
  • description — the short summary Google shows below the title link.

Edit the text, save, and rebuild the site (pnpm build).

How to add a new category

If you create a new quilt type in the future:

  1. Make a new folder here named after the category (e.g. t-shirt/).
  2. Inside it, create a file called _index.md with the same three fields shown above. Set slug to match the folder name.
  3. Rebuild the site (pnpm build) and check the result in your browser.
